passages.explanation: Paul leaves Athens and goes to Corinth — a wealthy, diverse, notoriously immoral port city. There he meets Aquila and Priscilla, a Jewish couple recently expelled from Rome by Claudius' edict against Jews. Because they share the same trade — tentmaking — Paul stays and works with them. Every Sabbath he reasons in the synagogue, trying to persuade both Jews and Greeks. Paul works with his hands to support himself. He doesn't want to be a financial burden, and in Corinth especially, he wants to distinguish himself from travelling philosophers who charged for their teaching.

When Silas and Timothy arrive from Macedonia, Paul devotes himself entirely to preaching, testifying to the Jews that Jesus is the Messiah. When they oppose him and become abusive, he shakes out his clothes in protest and says: your blood is on your own heads — I'm innocent. From now on I'll go to the Gentiles. He goes next door to the house of Titius Justus, a worshipper of God whose house is right next to the synagogue. Crispus, the synagogue leader, believes in the Lord along with his entire household. Many Corinthians hear, believe, and are baptised. Paul doesn't go far from the synagogue — literally next door. The gospel doesn't retreat; it relocates.

One night the Lord speaks to Paul in a vision: don't be afraid. Keep on speaking and don't be silent. I am with you, and no one is going to attack and harm you, because I have many people in this city. Paul stays in Corinth for a year and a half, teaching the word of God. The vision reveals that Paul needed encouragement — even the apostle to the Gentiles gets afraid. God's assurance isn't that there won't be opposition, but that Paul won't face it alone and the mission will bear fruit.

The Jews make a united attack on Paul and bring him before Gallio, the proconsul of Achaia. They accuse Paul of persuading people to worship God in ways contrary to the law. Before Paul can even speak, Gallio dismisses the case: if it were a matter of wrongdoing or serious crime, I'd listen. But since it involves questions about words and names and your own law, settle it yourselves — I refuse to be a judge of such things. He drives them from the court. Then the crowd seizes Sosthenes, the synagogue leader, and beats him in front of the court. Gallio shows no concern. The ruling is historically significant: a Roman proconsul has declared Christianity to be an internal Jewish matter, not a criminal offence. This sets a legal precedent that protects the church.

Paul stays on in Corinth for some time, then sails for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila. At Cenchreae he has his hair cut because of a vow — likely a Nazirite vow, showing Paul still practices Jewish customs. They arrive at Ephesus, where Paul goes to the synagogue and reasons with the Jews. They ask him to stay longer but he declines, saying he'll come back if God wills. He sails to Caesarea, goes up to Jerusalem to greet the church, and then goes to Antioch. After some time there, he sets out again through Galatia and Phrygia, strengthening all the disciples. The second missionary journey ends, and the third begins almost immediately. Paul is relentless.

Meanwhile, a Jew named Apollos arrives in Ephesus — a native of Alexandria, an eloquent man with thorough knowledge of the Scriptures. He's been instructed in the way of the Lord, speaks with great fervour, and teaches about Jesus accurately, though he knows only the baptism of John. He begins speaking boldly in the synagogue. Priscilla and Aquila hear him, take him aside, and explain the way of God more adequately. When Apollos wants to go to Achaia, the believers encourage him and write to the disciples to welcome him. He proves a great help there, vigorously refuting the Jews in public debate, proving from the Scriptures that Jesus is the Messiah. Apollos is already gifted and zealous — Priscilla and Aquila just fill in the gaps. Good mentoring doesn't replace someone's gifts; it completes them.
